features.name: Put every cache on one screen
features.desc: I counted seven cache layers in your own source — request, response, the data cache, the incremental one, the resumable one, and segment-cache twice, once on the client and once on the server — sitting next to a folder named use-cache and a doc literally called caching-without-cache-components. You shipped a migration guide for your own caching. That's the tell. Nobody holds seven invisible caches in their head, so a 'nothing changed' deploy quietly serves last week's data to a paying customer. Put it on one screen: what's cached, why, for how long, and the switch that clears it. A cache I can see is a cache I can trust.

features.name: One router. Ship the codemod. Hold the date.
features.desc: Open your own docs folder: 01-app and 02-pages. Two front doors, and every tutorial on earth has to pick one before it writes a line. That isn't choice you're handing people, it's a coin flip you make them do on day one. You already ship next-codemod, so make the move one command. Point at App, put a real date on Pages, and hold it. Two ways to do the one thing is the single most expensive line item you own.

features.name: Draw the server/client line on screen
features.desc: The words 'use client' appear seventy-three times inside your own framework, and you keep a TypeScript rule named client-boundary whose only job is policing that line. If your own 1,610-file codebase needs a linter to find it, every app does too — except they find it when it throws in production. Show the boundary instead: what runs on the server, what crosses to the client, what the crossing costs in kilobytes. A line I can see is a line I stop tripping over.

more.name: Collapse two build systems into one
more.desc: Your build folder ships webpack, webpack-build and webpack-config alongside turbopack-build and turbopack-analyze — two complete build systems you maintain in parallel and ask people to choose between. Turbopack is the future; say so out loud. Make it the default, keep webpack as the labeled escape hatch, and stop shipping two answers to 'how do I build.'

more.name: Cut next.config from sixty-three knobs to ten
more.desc: Sixty-three documented options. authInterrupts. htmlLimitedBots. instrumentationClientInject. mdxRs. Nobody remembers turning half of them on. Collapse it: ten options, each commented, defaults set to what you'd actually tell a friend to use. A config you can read in one sitting is a config you can trust.

more.name: Bless one way to fetch and mutate
more.desc: Server-component fetch, a server action (you ship action-handler for it), a route handler, unstable_cache — four answers to one question and a coin flip on every screen. Pick the one you'd put in front of a junior on day one, lead every doc with it, and let the rest be advanced moves people grow into.

more.name: Make dev behave like prod by default
more.desc: Caching off on my laptop and on in production is how a bug hides until it's live. The bug I can reproduce on my own machine is the one I fix in an hour instead of losing a weekend to. Make local match production out of the box, and let the difference be the thing I opt into, not the thing that ambushes me.

more.name: Errors that point at the line, not the layer
more.desc: Hydration mismatch, a stack trace, good luck. The framework already knows which component diverged and which prop changed, so say it, and name the one line to fix. The error you explain in plain words is the all-nighter nobody pulls.

killed: a fourth way to fetch data
telemetry you opt out of instead of into
one-cloud deploy assumptions baked into core

verdict.quote: I read your source this morning, not your marketing. Here is what I found. Two routers, numbered 01 and 02 in your own docs. Two build systems, webpack and turbopack, both shipped and both maintained. Seven caches. The words 'use client' seventy-three times inside your own framework. Sixty-three knobs in a config nobody finishes reading. You don't have a missing feature. You have two of everything, and a word — flexibility — that you use to get out of choosing. When I came back to Apple I cut three hundred and fifty products down to ten. People called it reckless. It was the only way the ten that were left got to be great. You are the best way to ship React. You are also drowning that under optionality. Pick one router. Pick one build. Show me the cache. Then delete the spare of everything and stop calling the leftovers a feature. Do that and you're the one nobody can argue with.
